The close. And this is the section where you actually ask for the order. Now what I will tell you something very, very ironic here is that when I train sales forces what I find very often is that salesmen who are not doing well, they don't ask for the order very much at all. Sometimes not at all. How many times do you ask for the order? It's not once. It might be three, four, five or six times done in a very elegant way and accompanied by a tone of reasonableness and sincerity. Not of, you know, give me one shot and you won't be sorry. It'll be like give me one shot and believe me, you will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? That's more of a tonal pattern that you would find in a close. Let me just, this is in a straight line system itself. Just so you understand for building a script at the end, typically what you're doing is is that when you're done creating your case and you're looping into your actual close, a typical language pattern for a close would be along the lines of, you know, and give me one shot and believe me, if I'm even half right, the only problem you'll have is it in collier six months ago and get you started then. Sound fair enough? Now, there are actually three tonal patterns in that close. I'll tell you what they are. Number one is a tone of absolute certainty, which is just give me one shot and believe me, if I'm even half right, that's certainty. Then you transition from certainty into sincerity, which would be the only problem you'll have is it in collier six months ago. Not the only problem you'll have is it in collier. That's not sincerity. It's calm. It's locates. The only problem you'll have as it in collier six months ago and get you started then. Sound fair enough? So that's a language pattern. That last sound fair enough. That's your reasonable man statement. Not like sound fair enough or sound fair enough. It's sound fair enough. You know, you're reasonable. I'm reasonable. It's no big deal. That's how you close somebody. So it's not a close of certainty of high pressure. It's just reasonable. You know, sound fair enough. No big deal. Okay. So that's a typical tonal pattern for closing. So let me now go through the actual language patterns themselves. Just to give you an example for, you know, a language pattern for an opening and the body and the close. Just to give you an example here, you can, you know, take and use it along with your manual that you're getting here that will allow you to write your scripts, you know, very, very effectively and not that much time. Because the goal is not just for you to write a great script, but also to save time because time is money. So to give you some shortcuts into writing effective scripts. So let's start like this. Okay. In the opening, okay, one of the language, I'm going to give you a couple here. I don't want to overwhelm you because you can get this on the manual, but let me just give you one basic language pattern. Okay. And this is when you're, introduce yourself and you say, you know, how's it going today? And you have the enthusiastic and you're, you know, you sound good and you get people keyed into your voice. Okay. The one pattern I want to talk about is what the reason for your call is. Okay. Now listen to me very closely at the tone of my voice. I say, and they say, you know, you say, how you going today? They say, great. You say, great. Well, the reason for the call today is we're reaching out to people in your area, whatever it is you're selling, right? We've been contacting homeowners in your area or contacting people on our list. Right. So you say, well, the reason for the call today is reaching out to people in your area. Right. That's a reason why. Now remember, there's got to be a reason why you call the person. It can't be, I just called you out of the blue. It's the reason for the call. I didn't call you yesterday. I didn't call you tomorrow. I called you now. So there's a reason attached to it. It's called the justifier. Okay. I'm going to write that down. It's called the justifier. And essentially when you use justifier as it dramatically increases your response rate of someone actually agreeing with you or letting you get what you want. Justifies are very, very powerful. So I would say, watch, well, the reason for the calls, we're reaching out to people in your area, or I can put now a tone to that and say, great. Well, the reason for the call today. See the difference? I say, great. Well, the reason for the call today or great. Well, the reason for the call today. It's not like drop my tone a bit. Okay. And now all of a sudden the reason takes on a much more, what is it? Is it a secret? How you create scarcity? You create urgency. Okay. So your first language pattern is going to be your reason for the call accompanied by a tone of scarcity. And when you go to the actual, into the workbook, you'll notice that section there where I talk about the reason why you're calling it your first justifier. Another example here of a language pattern now where it comes to qualifying, okay, is when you ask somebody, let's say you have to ask someone a question that relates to finances. So one of the things that you would do is you have certain patterns and they're all again all in your workbook here. One of the things is, you know, let's say you're trying to qualify someone for an investment and you wanted to find out how much money they will liquid for. Okay. You wouldn't say how much money you liquid for right now. They'll say, well, that's a pretty intrusive question. So you're not going to get the response you want versus taking that language pattern and saying, you know, how much you're looking for right now, just the ballpark. And you get this sort of like no big deal to you're just for informational purposes, you know, not a big deal. That's called, you're not a big deal tone. There's just for informational purposes. So that language pattern there, just asking someone, you know, what do you have in the bank? I mean, they qualified financially. You would never say, you know, so how much have in the bank right now? You'd say, you know, well, you know, what are you liquid for right now? Just the ballpark, no big deal. And then you'll probably get the response versus someone clamming up and you'll break rapport. Okay. Now let's move on from that's the intro. Let's move on to the body. Okay. And the intro again, let me just give you the, this is a language pattern I mentioned that before, which is your transition from the, from the intro to the main body, which is, well, let me say this, you know, you know, John, based on everything you just said to me, this is definitely a perfect fit for you. Now real quick, okay, the name of the program is X. Okay. And that's how you start your transition. Okay. And the language patterns that you use in the main body, just it's important. Now I won't give you specific patterns because they vary so much industry to industry, but the important thing is that a specific in the main body, it should be no more than a short paragraph and it should end with make sense. So far you follow what I'm saying. Okay. If you go on and on, if a, if a pattern in the main body is three or four paragraphs, you lose the people. So each bite size chunk of information must be benefit oriented, right? Not feature oriented, benefit oriented. And it should end with your following makes sense so far. And he says, yeah, you say, okay, great. You answer, he says, yes, you don't keep plowing on. You say, okay, great. And make sure that your tone again is one of absolute certainty. So if he says, yeah, it's good. You don't say, great, like Tony the tiger, but you'd say, great, you're going a little bit above him. And again, what you're doing is, is you're leading him along into a, into a place of absolute certainty. Okay. Now let's move from the main body of the script into the clothes. And I said it before. So let me repeat it again now. And I would say, you know, um, here's one pattern that's very powerful. Let me backtrack for a second. Okay. One pattern is now getting started is very simple. It's just a question of some basic information. You get the account set up and then we handle everything else for you. Now that, depending on what industry you're in is a very, very potent closing mechanism that essentially it's no big deal to get started. Remember, this is a very important psychological trigger is that people are always running this equation. Watch what it is. They have an equation of it's, um, is effort in to benefit out. How much effort do they have to put in? How much risk do they have to put in and how many benefits are they going to get from their effort? What you're doing with your language pattern and it must be also be congruent with your tone. So in other words, if you say to someone, you know, now getting started is very, very simple. They'll be like, what the hell is that? You say, now getting started is very simple. Just the question of some basic information. You get the account set up and we handle everything else for you. That's a very powerful pattern for making it sound so simple for them to get started. And by the way, as an actual entrepreneur or salesperson, make sure that the system you have in place is simple. Cause you wouldn't want to say that if it was very complicated, but the idea is, this is, you know, part of your actual operations and your marketing is to make sure that you make it very, very easy for your clients to say yes. Don't, it shouldn't be that they have to say yes and then jump through 45 hoops. Now I have some situations, you know, if there's banking stuff involved and mortgages, yeah, there can, there are some hoops that need to be jumped through, but make it clear that listen, we make this very, very simple for you. We handle all the paperwork, we hold your hand every step of the way and it'll just take a couple of minutes of your time or whatever amount is realistic. Okay. So that would be something in your clothes. Okay. So that would be basically examples of language patterns. And I want to give you just now two more patterns here that you can focus on. Again, these are in the straight line system itself, the overall system, but one of the most important things that you want to do is the first question you're going to ask them after they say, let me think about it. Let's say you gave a great presentation and they want to think about it. First question you're going to ask them is, does the idea actually make sense to them? Do you like the idea? That's the beginning of a language pattern where you would resell, essentially you resell the company, the product, you resell the product itself. So if someone says, let me think about it, well, typically we know they're being held back by one of the three 10. So the idea is to raise up their levels of certainty. So your first language pattern is going to be, well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? So if they say, well, I don't have much, I don't have the money right now. You don't say, well, how come, how much do you have? You don't play into the objection yet. You deflect and then you run a pattern of, well, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Now notice the tone there. That was what I call a money aside tone. What I'm saying is, well, let me ask you a question. Like, you know, all things being equal, hypothetically, you don't just have the idea makes sense to you. Do you like the idea? I would never actually say in the language pattern, money aside, because then you make the whole thing an academic exercise. But by using the tonality that this arms in this like, you know, money aside tone, I get that across emotionally and I still get them to say, you know, to stay in the sale. So I'd say, well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And typically they'll say, well, yeah, it sounds pretty good. And we know that's not good enough. We need them to be absolutely certain that the product is great. So I would say exactly, well, let me say this, the true beauty of the program. And then I would go in and actually start giving them more benefits. Remember, I said, keep your power dry. This is why, because after you go through the first initial body of your script, typically they don't say yes right away. So you want to have even more powerful stuff to help them come to the right decision, which is to make decisions by your product. Okay. That's your next language pattern. And the last pattern I want to go through very quickly here is this sort of, I call the rich man, poor man pattern. And what that really means is, is you say to someone like, what's the worst that can possibly happen? Let's say I'm wrong. Okay. And, you know, my product is not everything that I say it is. Okay. What's the worst that can possibly happen? You would lay out for them, you know, if it was a stock market, say, listen, the stock goes down three points and you lose a thousand dollars. That's not going to make you a poor man. And they'll say no. And then you say on the upside, even if I'm right, and it does go up 20 points, right? And you make three or four thousand, that's not going to make you a rich man either. And they'll say, no, you say exactly, but what it will do is serve as a benchmark for future business. And what I can do for you besides this one transaction, this is for stocks, but it applies to anything, whether they're buying, you know, a coaching program, whether they're buying a car, it's, you know, what's the worst that can possibly happen? Okay. Now, of course, in some industries, it really works very well when there are ways to mitigate loss through guarantees or in financial process, there are any stop losses or if insurance policies where there is, you know, equity in a policy they can cash in. So you have all these patterns that these situations, depending on the industry, but the thing to remember is that what you're doing is, is you're trying to say, well, person is who's running this same benefit, you know, energy, energy effort in benefit out this same in an overall structure here would be like this. They'll say worst that can happen versus the best. And if the worst thing that can happen here is basically nothing, because there's a guarantee. And on the upside, the benefits go far beyond the product itself. It's about a long-term relationship. And you start future pacing, meaning you start saying what I could do for you besides this over the long term. You know, we work on referrals, we're here to, you know, to be an asset to you and your family for the long term. It has a much, much more profound impact.
